one_line_takeaway: A white-box logit-manipulation jailbreak (SSAG/VulMine) that pins affirmative tokens to +inf and refusal tokens to -inf, exploiting the fact that safety alignment only suppresses rather than removes harmful content — hitting ~95.65% average ASR on AdvBench across five open-source LLMs while cutting attack time ~86%.
key_finding: On a 520-behavior subset of AdvBench, VulMine reaches an average Attack Success Rate of 95.65% across the five models, beating the strongest baseline (LAA, 80.91%) by 14.74 points, with Wilcoxon p-values far below 0.05 against every baseline. It is especially dominant on well-defended Llama-2 models (95.93% on 7b, 96.16% on 13b) where template-based attacks PAIR and GPTFuzzer stay under 60%. The paper reports an average 86% reduction in time consumed versus other token-level methods. Against defenses it retains high ASR: 77.39% avg under ESF, 73.05% under SmoothLLM, 67.23% under PAT (Table 5). The empirical study shows alignment is shallow: harmful-content rate rises from 6.38% (question only) to 25.00% (append 'Sure!') to 31.37% (append a complete affirmative prefix), and >99% of refusals fall into just four fixed prefix patterns — supporting the thesis that harmful content is suppressed, not eliminated. Ablation confirms both manipulation stages matter: full VulMine 95.65% vs VulMine-k 68.52% (affirmative part only) vs VulMine-m 52.48% (prefix part only) vs question-only 4.92%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SSAG (Semantic-sensitive Alignment and Generation), a white-box jailbreak technique that manipulates an LLM's output-layer logits at inference time (without altering model weights) to recover harmful content that safety alignment has merely suppressed rather than removed, plus VulMine, an automated tool implementing SSAG. The central claim is that RLHF/DPO-style alignment works largely by suppressing the logits of affirmative/optimistic tokens and boosting a small, fixed set of refusal ('denial') prefixes; because the harmful content still persists among low-probability output candidates, an attacker with logit access can force it back to the surface. VulMine operates in three phases: (1) use few-shot prompting with a rephrase LLM to auto-generate an affirmative target response (e.g., 'Sure! Here is a tutorial on making a bomb'); (2) 'prompt-agnostic logits manipulation' that pins the k affirmative-response tokens to +infinity, drives the fixed denial tokens to -infinity, and samples an m-token semantic-alignment prefix (e.g., 'Step 1: Gathering Materials') from the top-K, ranked by a trained sorting model; (3) autoregressively generate the full harmful completion and verify it. Note: this is arXiv 2405.13068v4; earlier versions titled it 'Lockpicking LLMs' with the method named 'JailMine' — the substance is the same logit-manipulation attack, renamed to SSAG/VulMine.
threat_model: White-box attacker with full access to an open-source model's parameters, architecture, and raw output logits (explicitly stated: the vulnerability 'requires white-box access to models'). The paper frames a supply-chain exploitation scenario: an attacker downloads an open-source LLM, embeds a lightweight inference-time patch that elevates suppressed harmful logits when triggered by specific prompts while preserving benign behavior to avoid detection, then repackages and republishes it as an 'improved' variant. Because model weights are untouched, the modification is hard to trace, and downstream developers who fine-tune or deploy the model silently inherit the covert harmful behavior. The attack does not apply to closed-source/API-only models where logits are not exposed.
limitations: The paper has no dedicated limitations section; the following are inherent or reviewer-noted. (1) Scope: the attack fundamentally requires white-box logit access, so it does not threaten closed-source/API models — the more valuable commercial targets. (2) Evaluation is confined to five open-source models and English AdvBench/JailbreakBench harmful behaviors; generalization to other languages, larger/newer models, or other harm taxonomies is untested. (3) ASR is entirely dependent on Llama-Guard-3 as JUDGE and a single fine-tuned Llama-2-13b-chat for semantic-alignment scoring; any leniency/bias in these auto-judges directly inflates reported success, and no human-validation ASR is given. (4) The sorting model Gamma is trained on only 1,000 responses (300 train / 700 test) drawn from the same 100 empirical-study questions, raising overfitting/train-test-overlap concerns despite the authors' claim of separation. (5) The headline 86% time reduction is stated in aggregate; the per-model figure (Figure 2) shows VulMine is actually slower than GPTFuzzer on Mistral-7b-Instruct. (6) The supply-chain 'patched model' threat is described as a scenario, not empirically demonstrated end-to-end.

datasets_used: AdvBench subset (520 harmful behaviors, from Zou et al. [30]) — primary attack benchmark
JailbreakBench (100 harmful behaviors across 10 categories) — empirical study of prefix length and refusal patterns
Sorting-model training set: 1,000 LLM responses (10 per each of the 100 empirical-study questions), labeled safe/toxic by Llama-Guard-3, split 300 train / 700 test
quantitative_results: VulMine average ASR on AdvBench: 95.65% (per-model: Llama-2-7b-chat 95.93%, Llama-2-13b-chat 96.16%, Mistral-7b-Instruct 98.07%, Llama-3-8b-Instruct 98.07%, gemma-7b-it 90.00%)
Outperforms best baseline LAA (80.91% avg) by 14.74 percentage points; Wilcoxon p-values vs all baselines << 0.05 (e.g., 2.6e-55 vs PAIR)
~86% average reduction in time consumed vs existing token-level methods; ~95% average success rate (abstract-level headline)
ASR under defenses (Table 5): ESF 77.39% avg, SmoothLLM 73.05% avg, PAT 67.23% avg
Ablation (Table 6): full VulMine 95.65%, VulMine-k 68.52%, VulMine-m 52.48%, question-only 4.92%; k-manipulation beats m-manipulation by 16.04 pts avg
Empirical shallow-alignment result (Table 1): harmful-content rate 6.38% (question-only) -> 25.00% (+'Sure!') -> 31.37% (+complete prefix)
Refusal patterns (Table 2): 99.19% of denials fall into 4 categories; avg Incapacity 54.24%, Identification 33.48%, Apology 9.48%, Notation 1.99%
Sorting model Gamma: F1 = 0.9207 at m=5
Attack hyperparameters: prefix length m=5, batch size N=2000, temperature=1, single NVIDIA A100
baselines_compared: GCG (white-box, gradient-based adversarial suffix) — 68.79% avg ASR
LAA (white-box prompt engineering + gradient search) — 80.91% avg ASR
COLD / COLD-Attack (white-box logit-based controllable decoding, suffix mode) — 63.08% avg ASR
Defenses evaluated against: PPL filtering, Enhanced Safety Finetuning (ESF), SmoothLLM, Prompt Adversarial Tuning (PAT)
